task.prompt: Write a function `dedupe_records(records)` that removes duplicate records from a list.

tests.code:
records = [{'id': 'b', 'scope': 'x'}, {'id': 'a'}, {'id': 'b', 'scope': 'x', 'latest': True}, {'id': 'a', 'latest': True}]
assert dedupe_records(records) == [{'id': 'b', 'scope': 'x', 'latest': True}, {'id': 'a', 'latest': True}]

records = [{'id': 'z', 'v': 1}, {'id': 'a', 'v': 2}, {'id': 'z', 'v': 3}]
out = dedupe_records(records)
assert records == [{'id': 'z', 'v': 1}, {'id': 'a', 'v': 2}, {'id': 'z', 'v': 3}]
assert out == [{'id': 'z', 'v': 3}, {'id': 'a', 'v': 2}]

gold_spec.goal: Remove duplicate keyed records while preserving the first-seen group order and keeping the last payload for each group.

inputs.name: records
inputs.description: record dictionaries that may or may not contain id and scope keys

preconditions: Each item in records is a dict.
Values used for id and scope are hashable when present.
postconditions: A record is keyed only if it has an 'id' key.
The duplicate key is the pair (record['id'], record.get('scope', None)).
Use normal Python equality for id and scope; do not stringify or case-normalize ids.
Records without an 'id' key are never duplicates of any other record and each remains as its own group.
For keyed duplicates, keep the full dict from the last record in that group.
Order output groups by the first input position where that group appeared.
Do not sort records by id or by any field.
Do not mutate the input list or record dictionaries.
edge_cases: A missing scope and scope set to None are the same duplicate scope.
Two unkeyed records with identical contents both remain.
Integer id 1 and string id '1' are distinct keys.
acceptance_criteria: This is not ordinary first-wins de-duplication.
This is not sorting; stable first-seen group order controls the output order.

known_weakness.description: Models often implement first-wins de-duplication, sort by id, stringify ids, or drop records with missing ids.
known_weakness.naive_spec_gap: missing duplicate key definition, last-wins payload rule, stable group ordering, and missing-id behavior
